text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29A.1	The Department of Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance is responsible for policy leadership in all management areas through the provision of a clear, integrated global management strategy and policy framework and through strengthened monitoring, evaluation and accountability mechanisms. The Department is also responsible, jointly with the Department of Operational Support, for managing the activities of the Office of Information and Communications Technology. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 72/266 A, 72/266 B, 72/254 and 73/281. The Department plays an integral role in realizing the Secretary-General’s management paradigm, with an emphasis on transparency, accountability and decision-making that is closer to points of delivery.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29A.2	The strategy for 2027 includes continuing to advance the management paradigm through an integrated approach, with five interlinked pillars: the enterprise resource planning solution; programme planning, finance and budget; human resources; business transformation and accountability; and addressing racism and racial discrimination. The Department will:

(a)	Commit to new internal ways of working, including through the formulation of holistic policies and approaches that foster the achievement of tangible results, including through renewed investment in technology and a commitment to continuous improvement;
(b)	Strengthen the Organization’s resilience, including its preparedness to maintain business continuity in emergencies and to continually adapt to changing environments, risks and opportunities;

(c)	Drive results-based management policies and practices into the daily functioning and culture of the Organization, together with an integrated cycle of planning, risk and performance management, and evaluation;
(d)	Promote a culture of efficiency in the request for and use of Member States’ resources for the implementation of all Secretariat activities;

(e)	Foster greater transparency and accountability to engender greater credibility and trust in the capabilities of the United Nations as an organization, among both the Member States and the people whom it serves;

(f)	Enhance data analysis and reporting capabilities that enable real-time, user-friendly access to data to support informed decision-making and improved management of risks to achieve the objectives of the United Nations;
(g)	Create a collaborative and inclusive environment through integrated solutions and strategic partnerships throughout the United Nations system;

(h)	Work towards the implementation of a dynamic human resources strategy that achieves greater workforce diversity, supports the development and integration of skills and capabilities required to fulfil mandates and respond effectively to current and future challenges, supports managers in finding the right people, provides a strengthened learning framework and a strengthened multilingualism framework, ensures a civil and safe work environment and promotes a culture of continuous learning, innovation and improvement to actively build a better United Nations;

(i)	Address racism and racial discrimination more directly and effectively in the Secretariat by encouraging reporting; enhancing accountability, monitoring and transparency; and periodically reporting on efforts and progress towards addressing racism and promoting dignity for all.

29A.3	The Department will continue to provide leadership in the development and delivery of integrated organizational management strategies, policies and models supporting a decentralized, field-focused paradigm in which managers are empowered through the enhanced delegation of authority framework. It will liaise with Member States and other external entities on management-related issues and foster coordination within the Secretariat, as well as with the funds, programmes and specialized agencies of the United Nations system, to build partnerships and promote best practices.

29A.4	Having laid the foundation for greater decentralization, the main challenge will continue to be to maintain the culture of increased delegation of authority, transparency and accountability and a culture of continuous learning.

29A.5	The Department will continue to enable business transformation that leverages current and future trends in enterprise resource planning solutions. The Umoja solution and its accompanying business intelligence framework will allow the Department to respond to organizational requirements for better and more timely decision-making, enhanced accountability systems and the rapid adoption of newly identified best practices, as well as strengthened support for real-time data access, augmented and predictive analytics and planning and monitoring capabilities.

29A.6	With regard to cooperation with other entities, the Department will continue to support and work closely with all entities of the Secretariat and build partnerships, internally within the Secretariat and externally with the United Nations system, to find inclusive responses to challenges and develop innovative business models that cater to the evolving and specific requirements of each entity, with an emphasis on the field.

29A.7	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will represent the Secretary-General in inter-agency coordination mechanisms, including the High-level Committee on Management of the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination (CEB).

29A.8	With regard to the external factors, the Department considered applicable risks identified in its risk register, and, accordingly, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that all stakeholders will extend their full cooperation and support to the Department in implementing its mandates.

29A.9	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. The Department will play a leadership role in driving the Secretary-General’s system-wide strategy on the future of work and continue to support efforts to achieve gender parity and address sexual harassment in the workplace. Furthermore, the Department will work towards the equitable geographical distribution of Member States in the Secretariat workforce and prioritize the integration of environmental sustainability and resilience management into the strategies, policies and compliance and reporting frameworks of the Organization. Through its policy leadership in these interconnected fields, the Department will foster a change in culture at all levels of the Secretariat.

29A.10	The Department will monitor, coordinate and support implementation of the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and will continue to elevate awareness of disability inclusion within the Secretariat and support entities by mainstreaming disability inclusion into policies, programmes and human resources strategy, in particular in the areas of leadership, capacity-building, enabling work environment and inclusive workforce.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

29A.11	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

29A.12	The Department will be guided by any decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, decisions related to improving Secretariat support for Member States during mandate creation, implementation and review.
Legislative mandates
29A.13	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.

children.text: Evaluation activities

29A.15	A review of donor-led assessments of United Nations system organizations and other oversight-related requests from donors in the context of funding agreements and the United Nations single audit principle, conducted by the Joint Inspection Unit and completed in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

29A.16	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, specifically a recommendation to ensure the explicit presentation of the Organization’s assurance mechanisms in contribution agreements, subprogramme 2, component 1 has established that contribution agreements should routinely include standard provisions on auditing, evaluation, monitoring and reporting. This provides assurances to donors that funds are spent by the United Nations in accordance with its legal and regulatory framework and consistent with the terms of the contribution agreements.
29A.17	No evaluation is planned for 2027 as the Department plans to undertake an in-depth internal evaluation every two years.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Enterprise resource planning solution, services to the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly and to the Committee for Programme and Coordination, and management advisory services
Component 1 – Enterprise resource planning solution – Objective
29A.18	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to improve the management of the Organization’s resources for its work in line with mandates.
Strategy
29A.19	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Support business transformation in the areas of programme planning, programme management, the monitoring of programme delivery, and data and business analytics by harmonizing processes and practices and using modern technology;

(b)	Support the Organization by building on its business intelligence platforms to support access to real-time data, augmented and predictive analytics, including machine learning and natural language processing, and planning capabilities;

(c)	Support, monitor and upgrade the Umoja system to meet evolving business needs through continuous improvements directed by the business and work with business areas, to translate requirements into integrated functional solutions that are built, whenever possible, through out-of-the-box solutions that have sustainable road maps and meet Umoja architectural standards.
29A.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more efficient and transparent management of the Organization’s financial, human and physical resources;
(b)	Effective and timely decision-making by management informed by advanced analytics;
(c)	Rapid adoption and application of newly identified best practices by the Organization;
(d)	The continued effective operation of the Organization given that Umoja functionality will underpin the Organization’s work.
29A.32	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Improve communication on organizational and procedural aspects of meetings;
(b)	Enhance substantive, technical and Secretariat support to Member States and other participants in the meetings.
29A.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the smooth conduct of meetings of the intergovernmental bodies serviced.
29A.45	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Strengthen the Organization’s resilience, including its preparedness to maintain business continuity in emergencies and to continually adapt to changing environments, risks and opportunities;
(b)	Improve the overall quality of the acquisition process and reflect those improvements in substantive policy and guidance-related recommendations, including through the use of data analytics;

(c)	Objectively analyse the facts presented in requests for a management evaluation vis-à-vis the legal framework of the Staff Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and provide the requesting staff member with a reasoned, comprehensive evaluation conveying the basis for the recommendation of the Management Advice and Evaluation Section on the case.
29A.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more resilient, agile and sustainable Organization and an innovative and thriving workforce that delivers results;
(b)	An Organization better prepared for complex and long-term emergencies;
(c)	A strengthened acquisition and asset disposal process;
(d)	The early resolution of disputes that arise from decisions taken in the Administration on a staff member’s employment;
(e)	Greater staff awareness of the internal justice system.
Programme performance in 2025
Digital transformation using Umoja for improved efficiency and effective decision-making

29A.21	Building on the completion of the Umoja enhancement package 8 upgrade project, the Umoja mobile platform was upgraded in May 2025, providing a modern interface, expanded functionality and an improved user experience. The resilience of the Umoja system was also enhanced through a high-availability solution and full hardware symmetry.

children.text: Supported Fifth Committee processes through expanded information and efficient reporting

29A.34	In 2025, the component, based on lessons learned and taking into consideration feedback received from delegations, continued its efforts to simplify documentation and expand access to information, including through the Fifth Committee website, e-deleGATE and iGov. The issuance of a new document on the organization of the work of the Fifth Committee at the eightieth session of the General Assembly provided consolidated information and sources. In parallel with other Main Committees, and with a view to enhancing efficiency, the component consolidated the Fifth Committee’s reporting to the General Assembly on the recommendations adopted at the closing meeting of the main part of the eightieth session into a single report, thereby reducing from 15 the number of documents covering that meeting.
29A.35	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.I).
Figure 29A.I
Performance measure: Member States’ delegates who were either “very satisfied” or “satisfied” with the availability of information and documents prepared (annual)
(Percentage)
Timely responses to management evaluation requests

29A.47	Over the final three months of 2025, the Management Advice and Evaluation Section received over 50 per cent of its total caseload for the year, marking a significant increase in the number of cases received during such a short period. This included 127 cases concerning a policy change to roster management. Given the unusually heavy caseload in 2025 and the need to ensure the timely completion of all case management evaluations, the subprogramme conducted a review of its internal case management workflow and was able to expedite its evaluation of the increased workload in order to meet the statutory deadline in all of these cases.

children.text: Result 3: upgrade to the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.27	Foundational work for the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) upgrade project started in 2025 and will continue through 2029 to meet the software support deadlines. This upgrade will be one of the Secretariat’s most significant system upgrades to date, with its scale and complexity comparable to the original Umoja implementation.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.28	The lesson for the component was that, as the software support for the current version of Umoja expires in 2030, the NextGen ERP upgrade project will have to be prioritized. In applying the lesson, this upgrade will be cross-cutting across all functional areas and will require seamless collaboration across all stakeholders.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened support on election- and appointment-related processes – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.40	In implementing its activities in support of the Fifth Committee’s work in the areas of appointments and elections, the component continues to prepare the relevant parliamentary documentation, disseminate information and facilitate the submission and processing of nominations and endorsements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.41	The lesson for the component was that feedback from delegations underscored the importance of the component providing clear and targeted information on appointments and elections in support of the Committee. The component has, for example, consolidated information previously included across separate documents and different formats into a single, more user-friendly document and expanded the scope of the e-Candidatures module to future election cycles. In applying the lesson, the component will continue to enhance the information provided to Member States and further facilitate their easy and secure use of the e-Candidatures module for submitting nominations and endorsements.

29A.51	The component’s work contributed to the delegated officials accepting 98 per cent of the recommendations of the Headquarters Committee on Contracts, which exceeded the planned target of 97 per cent.

Performance measure: percentage of recommendations accepted by delegated officials (annual)
Result 3: Enhanced understanding by staff of the Organization’s formal system of justice – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.53	The component conducts a preliminary review of management evaluation requests to determine their procedural receivability, that is, whether a request has been submitted within the statutory deadlines, whether the contested decision is appealable under the Staff Rules and whether the requestor has standing to appeal. The component noted that a significant number of cases over the past three years had been determined to be not receivable (36 per cent in 2023, 28 per cent in 2024 and 34 per cent in 2025).
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.54	The lesson for the component was that, while assessing receivability is often not straightforward, staff in a portion of those requests could have benefited from a greater background understanding of the procedural issues involved. In applying the lesson, the component will, in addition to its outreach briefings, elaborate further guidance on these issues to assist staff in deciding whether to exercise their recourse in the Organization’s formal system of justice.

children.text: Component 2 – Services to the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly and to the Committee for Programme and Coordination – Objective

29A.31	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to facilitate effective and efficient deliberations and decision-making by the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly and the Committee for Programme and Coordination.
Strategy
29A.19	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Support business transformation in the areas of programme planning, programme management, the monitoring of programme delivery, and data and business analytics by harmonizing processes and practices and using modern technology;

(b)	Support the Organization by building on its business intelligence platforms to support access to real-time data, augmented and predictive analytics, including machine learning and natural language processing, and planning capabilities;

(c)	Support, monitor and upgrade the Umoja system to meet evolving business needs through continuous improvements directed by the business and work with business areas, to translate requirements into integrated functional solutions that are built, whenever possible, through out-of-the-box solutions that have sustainable road maps and meet Umoja architectural standards.
29A.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more efficient and transparent management of the Organization’s financial, human and physical resources;
(b)	Effective and timely decision-making by management informed by advanced analytics;
(c)	Rapid adoption and application of newly identified best practices by the Organization;
(d)	The continued effective operation of the Organization given that Umoja functionality will underpin the Organization’s work.
29A.32	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Improve communication on organizational and procedural aspects of meetings;
(b)	Enhance substantive, technical and Secretariat support to Member States and other participants in the meetings.
29A.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the smooth conduct of meetings of the intergovernmental bodies serviced.
29A.45	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Strengthen the Organization’s resilience, including its preparedness to maintain business continuity in emergencies and to continually adapt to changing environments, risks and opportunities;
(b)	Improve the overall quality of the acquisition process and reflect those improvements in substantive policy and guidance-related recommendations, including through the use of data analytics;

(c)	Objectively analyse the facts presented in requests for a management evaluation vis-à-vis the legal framework of the Staff Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and provide the requesting staff member with a reasoned, comprehensive evaluation conveying the basis for the recommendation of the Management Advice and Evaluation Section on the case.
29A.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more resilient, agile and sustainable Organization and an innovative and thriving workforce that delivers results;
(b)	An Organization better prepared for complex and long-term emergencies;
(c)	A strengthened acquisition and asset disposal process;
(d)	The early resolution of disputes that arise from decisions taken in the Administration on a staff member’s employment;
(e)	Greater staff awareness of the internal justice system.
Programme performance in 2025
Digital transformation using Umoja for improved efficiency and effective decision-making

29A.21	Building on the completion of the Umoja enhancement package 8 upgrade project, the Umoja mobile platform was upgraded in May 2025, providing a modern interface, expanded functionality and an improved user experience. The resilience of the Umoja system was also enhanced through a high-availability solution and full hardware symmetry.

children.text: Supported Fifth Committee processes through expanded information and efficient reporting

29A.34	In 2025, the component, based on lessons learned and taking into consideration feedback received from delegations, continued its efforts to simplify documentation and expand access to information, including through the Fifth Committee website, e-deleGATE and iGov. The issuance of a new document on the organization of the work of the Fifth Committee at the eightieth session of the General Assembly provided consolidated information and sources. In parallel with other Main Committees, and with a view to enhancing efficiency, the component consolidated the Fifth Committee’s reporting to the General Assembly on the recommendations adopted at the closing meeting of the main part of the eightieth session into a single report, thereby reducing from 15 the number of documents covering that meeting.
29A.35	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.I).
Figure 29A.I
Performance measure: Member States’ delegates who were either “very satisfied” or “satisfied” with the availability of information and documents prepared (annual)
(Percentage)
Timely responses to management evaluation requests

29A.47	Over the final three months of 2025, the Management Advice and Evaluation Section received over 50 per cent of its total caseload for the year, marking a significant increase in the number of cases received during such a short period. This included 127 cases concerning a policy change to roster management. Given the unusually heavy caseload in 2025 and the need to ensure the timely completion of all case management evaluations, the subprogramme conducted a review of its internal case management workflow and was able to expedite its evaluation of the increased workload in order to meet the statutory deadline in all of these cases.

children.text: Result 3: upgrade to the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.27	Foundational work for the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) upgrade project started in 2025 and will continue through 2029 to meet the software support deadlines. This upgrade will be one of the Secretariat’s most significant system upgrades to date, with its scale and complexity comparable to the original Umoja implementation.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.28	The lesson for the component was that, as the software support for the current version of Umoja expires in 2030, the NextGen ERP upgrade project will have to be prioritized. In applying the lesson, this upgrade will be cross-cutting across all functional areas and will require seamless collaboration across all stakeholders.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened support on election- and appointment-related processes – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.40	In implementing its activities in support of the Fifth Committee’s work in the areas of appointments and elections, the component continues to prepare the relevant parliamentary documentation, disseminate information and facilitate the submission and processing of nominations and endorsements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.41	The lesson for the component was that feedback from delegations underscored the importance of the component providing clear and targeted information on appointments and elections in support of the Committee. The component has, for example, consolidated information previously included across separate documents and different formats into a single, more user-friendly document and expanded the scope of the e-Candidatures module to future election cycles. In applying the lesson, the component will continue to enhance the information provided to Member States and further facilitate their easy and secure use of the e-Candidatures module for submitting nominations and endorsements.

Figure 29A.II
Performance measure: percentage of recommendations accepted by delegated officials (annual)
Result 3: Enhanced understanding by staff of the Organization’s formal system of justice – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.53	The component conducts a preliminary review of management evaluation requests to determine their procedural receivability, that is, whether a request has been submitted within the statutory deadlines, whether the contested decision is appealable under the Staff Rules and whether the requestor has standing to appeal. The component noted that a significant number of cases over the past three years had been determined to be not receivable (36 per cent in 2023, 28 per cent in 2024 and 34 per cent in 2025).
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.54	The lesson for the component was that, while assessing receivability is often not straightforward, staff in a portion of those requests could have benefited from a greater background understanding of the procedural issues involved. In applying the lesson, the component will, in addition to its outreach briefings, elaborate further guidance on these issues to assist staff in deciding whether to exercise their recourse in the Organization’s formal system of justice.

children.text: Component 3 – Management advisory services – Objective

29A.44	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to advance the transparency of administrative decision-making, fairness, integrity and the resilience of the Organization to deliver on its mandates.
Strategy
29A.19	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Support business transformation in the areas of programme planning, programme management, the monitoring of programme delivery, and data and business analytics by harmonizing processes and practices and using modern technology;

(b)	Support the Organization by building on its business intelligence platforms to support access to real-time data, augmented and predictive analytics, including machine learning and natural language processing, and planning capabilities;

(c)	Support, monitor and upgrade the Umoja system to meet evolving business needs through continuous improvements directed by the business and work with business areas, to translate requirements into integrated functional solutions that are built, whenever possible, through out-of-the-box solutions that have sustainable road maps and meet Umoja architectural standards.
29A.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more efficient and transparent management of the Organization’s financial, human and physical resources;
(b)	Effective and timely decision-making by management informed by advanced analytics;
(c)	Rapid adoption and application of newly identified best practices by the Organization;
(d)	The continued effective operation of the Organization given that Umoja functionality will underpin the Organization’s work.
29A.32	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Improve communication on organizational and procedural aspects of meetings;
(b)	Enhance substantive, technical and Secretariat support to Member States and other participants in the meetings.
29A.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the smooth conduct of meetings of the intergovernmental bodies serviced.
29A.45	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Strengthen the Organization’s resilience, including its preparedness to maintain business continuity in emergencies and to continually adapt to changing environments, risks and opportunities;
(b)	Improve the overall quality of the acquisition process and reflect those improvements in substantive policy and guidance-related recommendations, including through the use of data analytics;

(c)	Objectively analyse the facts presented in requests for a management evaluation vis-à-vis the legal framework of the Staff Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and provide the requesting staff member with a reasoned, comprehensive evaluation conveying the basis for the recommendation of the Management Advice and Evaluation Section on the case.
29A.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A more resilient, agile and sustainable Organization and an innovative and thriving workforce that delivers results;
(b)	An Organization better prepared for complex and long-term emergencies;
(c)	A strengthened acquisition and asset disposal process;
(d)	The early resolution of disputes that arise from decisions taken in the Administration on a staff member’s employment;
(e)	Greater staff awareness of the internal justice system.
Programme performance in 2025
Digital transformation using Umoja for improved efficiency and effective decision-making

29A.21	Building on the completion of the Umoja enhancement package 8 upgrade project, the Umoja mobile platform was upgraded in May 2025, providing a modern interface, expanded functionality and an improved user experience. The resilience of the Umoja system was also enhanced through a high-availability solution and full hardware symmetry.
29A.22	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.2).

children.text: Supported Fifth Committee processes through expanded information and efficient reporting

29A.34	In 2025, the component, based on lessons learned and taking into consideration feedback received from delegations, continued its efforts to simplify documentation and expand access to information, including through the Fifth Committee website, e-deleGATE and iGov. The issuance of a new document on the organization of the work of the Fifth Committee at the eightieth session of the General Assembly provided consolidated information and sources. In parallel with other Main Committees, and with a view to enhancing efficiency, the component consolidated the Fifth Committee’s reporting to the General Assembly on the recommendations adopted at the closing meeting of the main part of the eightieth session into a single report, thereby reducing from 15 the number of documents covering that meeting.
29A.35	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.I).
Figure 29A.I
Performance measure: Member States’ delegates who were either “very satisfied” or “satisfied” with the availability of information and documents prepared (annual)
(Percentage)
Timely responses to management evaluation requests

29A.47	Over the final three months of 2025, the Management Advice and Evaluation Section received over 50 per cent of its total caseload for the year, marking a significant increase in the number of cases received during such a short period. This included 127 cases concerning a policy change to roster management. Given the unusually heavy caseload in 2025 and the need to ensure the timely completion of all case management evaluations, the subprogramme conducted a review of its internal case management workflow and was able to expedite its evaluation of the increased workload in order to meet the statutory deadline in all of these cases.

children.text: Result 3: upgrade to the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.27	Foundational work for the Next Generation Enterprise Resource Planning (NextGen ERP) upgrade project started in 2025 and will continue through 2029 to meet the software support deadlines. This upgrade will be one of the Secretariat’s most significant system upgrades to date, with its scale and complexity comparable to the original Umoja implementation.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.28	The lesson for the component was that, as the software support for the current version of Umoja expires in 2030, the NextGen ERP upgrade project will have to be prioritized. In applying the lesson, this upgrade will be cross-cutting across all functional areas and will require seamless collaboration across all stakeholders.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened support on election- and appointment-related processes – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.40	In implementing its activities in support of the Fifth Committee’s work in the areas of appointments and elections, the component continues to prepare the relevant parliamentary documentation, disseminate information and facilitate the submission and processing of nominations and endorsements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.41	The lesson for the component was that feedback from delegations underscored the importance of the component providing clear and targeted information on appointments and elections in support of the Committee. The component has, for example, consolidated information previously included across separate documents and different formats into a single, more user-friendly document and expanded the scope of the e-Candidatures module to future election cycles. In applying the lesson, the component will continue to enhance the information provided to Member States and further facilitate their easy and secure use of the e-Candidatures module for submitting nominations and endorsements.

Result 3: Enhanced understanding by staff of the Organization’s formal system of justice – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.53	The component conducts a preliminary review of management evaluation requests to determine their procedural receivability, that is, whether a request has been submitted within the statutory deadlines, whether the contested decision is appealable under the Staff Rules and whether the requestor has standing to appeal. The component noted that a significant number of cases over the past three years had been determined to be not receivable (36 per cent in 2023, 28 per cent in 2024 and 34 per cent in 2025).
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.54	The lesson for the component was that, while assessing receivability is often not straightforward, staff in a portion of those requests could have benefited from a greater background understanding of the procedural issues involved. In applying the lesson, the component will, in addition to its outreach briefings, elaborate further guidance on these issues to assist staff in deciding whether to exercise their recourse in the Organization’s formal system of justice.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Programme planning, finance and budget
Component 1 – Finance – Objective

29A.57	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to secure the financing of the Organization’s expenses, pursuant to Article 17 of the Charter of the United Nations, and to ensure sound, effective and efficient integrated financial management of and reporting on United Nations resources, supported by a strong internal control environment.
Strategy
29A.58	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Ensure the proper application of the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, adapt policies relating to financial matters in response to emerging issues and strengthen the system of internal controls in support of the efficient management of resources in the context of the decentralized delegation of authority framework;

(b)	Provide effective support to the Committee on Contributions, ensure the timely issuance of assessments, provide up-to-date information on the status of assessed contributions and closely monitor and forecast the collection of assessments and manage cash outflows on the basis of liquidity forecasts;

(c)	Prepare International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS)-compliant financial statements for the Organization, focusing on transparency, by incorporating a financial discussion and an analysis statement, as well as ensuring that updates to accounting standards are operationalized and that all entities are appropriately guided in year-end requirements for the preparation of financial statements and are supported in the establishment, management and reporting of the extrabudgetary funds of the Organization;
(d)	Manage financial risks through sound financial processes, investment management, global cash management and global banking management;

(e)	Coordinate and manage the group health and life insurance programmes and provide services to the Advisory Board on Compensation Claims and the United Nations Claims Board, and administer related compensation payments;
(f)	Provide governance of finance master data to ensure the integrity, standardization and harmonization of data across the enterprise resource planning system;
(g)	Provide procedures, guidance and support for Umoja finance processes to ensure effective and efficient financial management.
29A.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Prudent financial management of the resources entrusted to the Secretariat;
(b)	A strengthened system of internal controls that supports better programme performance management and compliance with the regulatory framework;
(c)	Increased transparency and simplification of financial processes and reporting;
(d)	Effective and efficient financial procedures;
(e)	Better positioning of the Organization to respond to external shocks.

29A.71	To contribute to the objective, the component will provide strategic guidance on budgeting and financial stewardship and develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard, in particular:

(a)	Provide strategic guidance on financial management and financing issues, especially on cross-cutting initiatives affecting field operations, including guidance on formulating human resources requirements, corporate initiatives, financial strength and liquidity and strengthening of internal controls, and timely, high-quality, simplified and action-oriented responses to managers in the field; monitor the liquidity of individual special accounts; and oversee cash-flow and operating reserves for field operations;
(b)	Provide strategic guidance on global property management and real estate services, including oversight of major construction projects and long-term capital planning;

(c)	Provide expert advice and guidance on property management and review financial data for accurate recording and for compliance with IPSAS and the established property management performance framework for Secretariat entities, and ensure the central supervision of projects, including by incorporating risk management principles and lessons learned in decision-making.

29A.72	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in client entities effectively and efficiently managing their resources within the approved levels, with improved accuracy in forecasts and proposals.
29A.85	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Issue strategic guidance on the preparation of the proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals and engage with Member States from the early stages of the budget process;

(b)	Finalize and present to the legislative bodies the Secretary-General’s annual proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals, the budget performance reports and other reports on budgetary matters, including statements of programme budget implications and revised programme budget proposals, in accordance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, the Regulations and Rules Governing Programme Planning, the Programme Aspects of the Budget, the Monitoring of Implementation and the Methods of Evaluation and applicable resolutions of the General Assembly;

(c)	Provide information to Member States on the potential budgetary implications of draft resolutions of the General Assembly and its Main Committees, the Economic and Social Council, the Human Rights Council and other legislative organs;

(d)	Provide substantive services on programme planning and budgetary matters to the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly, the Committee for Programme and Coordination and the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions and provide online tools accessible to Member States in order to facilitate deliberations;

(e)	Provide strategic guidance on results-based management, budgeting and financial stewardship, including on managing spending authorities, develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard and provide advice on all budgetary matters.
29A.86	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased transparency and strengthened dialogue between the Secretariat and Member States;
(b)	Efficient and effective implementation of programmes while ensuring the prudent use of resources;
(c)	Improved accuracy of programme budgets.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved access to consolidated cost-recovery information for decision-making by Secretariat entities

29A.60	Cost-recovery activities across the Secretariat are recorded across multiple Umoja modules, including funds management, accounts receivable, general ledger, controlling, sales and distribution, and payroll. The absence of integrated reporting meant that entities relied on fragmented data sources and manual extraction, making it difficult to efficiently monitor fund balances, track revenue, assess receivables or evaluate performance. Following repeated recommendations from oversight bodies for strengthened monitoring, the component led a multi-year effort culminating in 2025 with the deployment of a cost-recovery dashboard in Umoja Analytics. The dashboard integrates data across six modules into a single platform, providing consolidated analysis of fund balance movements, revenue by client and cost-recovery method, receivables ageing and payment patterns, and budget consumption against revenue and cash collected. The Assistant Secretary-General for Programme Planning, Finance and Budget, Controller, as well as heads of entities, service providers and clients can now access streamlined, actionable cost-recovery insights from their own operational perspective, replacing what previously required consulting multiple disconnected reports.
29A.61	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.15).

children.text: Strengthened oversight and accountability in property management

29A.73	In 2025, the component strengthened the organizational performance management framework through the establishment of a consolidated data quality report, underpinned by key performance indicators covering critical data fields, to support entities in identifying data inconsistencies in property records. The indicators focused on the accuracy and timeliness of Umoja data, including the correct reflection of property status across the lifecycle, such as assets issued into operational use versus those held in warehouse, thereby reinforcing accountability for data quality at the entity level.

29A.74	Introduced towards the end of the year, the data quality report provided initial visibility over areas of potential inefficiency in property management processes and laid the foundation for more targeted corrective actions by entities. The framework supported early identification of data gaps, strengthened oversight and reporting and established a basis for more informed management decision-making going forward.

children.text: Successful conclusion of budgetary deliberations on the UN80 initiative within a condensed timeline

29A.87	Following the launch of the UN80 Initiative by the Secretary-General in March 2025 to build a stronger and more effective Organization that is better equipped to deliver on its mandates, the report on the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period (A/80/400) was prepared and presented for review by legislative bodies in a condensed period. The proposals resulted from the engagement of all Secretariat entities led and coordinated by the subprogramme, in particular the component, in its lead role for the programme budget. The General Assembly successfully concluded its deliberations on 30 December 2025.

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved client experience with United Nations global payment systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29A.62	The component’s work contributed to 1,850 individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment, exceeding the planned target of 1,000 clients.
29A.63	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.III).
Figure 29A.III
Performance measure: number of individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment (annual)
Result 2: implementation of approved updates to the International Public Sector Accounting Standards and impact analysis of upcoming changes – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29A.64	The component’s work in integrated system enhancements, authoritative policy documents, Organization-wide lease information capture and a comprehensive guidance and training suite ensured effective implementation of significant IPSAS updates in 2025 and contributed to full audit assurance and no audit recommendations raised during the year, which met the planned target.
29A.65	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.IV).
Figure 29A.IV
Performance measure: number of audit recommendations related to implementation of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (annual)
Note: The financial statements of the United Nations, volume I, for the year ended 31 December 2025 will be audited in April and May 2026.
Result 3: strengthened financial management regulatory framework through newly established and updated policy instruments – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.66	As part of ongoing efforts to strengthen the financial management regulatory framework, promote consistent understanding and application of policies and enhance accountability across Secretariat operations, the component has been conducting a comprehensive review of a compendium of policy instruments, with a view to issuing new and updated key policies and guidelines as applicable.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.67	The lesson for the component was the importance of ensuring that entities receive appropriate technical support and training related to policy updates to ensure harmonization and correct common understanding of the instruments. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will develop and issue additional operational guidelines on an ongoing basis, incorporating feedback received from entities, and provide continuous training to support consistent and effective implementation. This will further support the effective management of financial resources and provide the necessary flexibility in complex operations.

children.text: Result 3: greater transparency and efficiency in the formulation of budgetary requirements for special political missions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.80	The component makes efforts to identify efficiency gains in the budgetary preparation process for the special political missions while ensuring effective mandate implementation, including by applying a data-driven approach in formulating budgetary requirements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.81	The lesson for the component was the importance of building and strengthening a culture of efficiency for the use of resources for the implementation of mandates. In applying the lesson, the component will, in line with General Assembly resolution 80/243, facilitate mutual learning across missions in identifying, tracking, quantifying and reporting on efficiency initiatives across post and non-post resources. To enhance reporting, reports of the Secretary-General will include additional information on efficiency gains, including a comparison of projected efficiencies made at the outset of the budget period and the actual realized efficiencies.

children.text: Result 1: enhanced transparency on the programme budget implications process for Member States – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29A.89	The component’s work contributed to Member States from four Main Committees having access to information at the start of their session on the programme budget implications process through a detailed guide available on the portal, which did not meet the planned target of Member States from six Main Committees having access to information. The target was not met because resources had to be prioritized to support operational requirements arising from the UN80 reforms, particularly the preparation of the report on the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period (A/80/400).

children.text: Result 3: greater efficiency reflected in budgetary requirements across Secretariat entities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.93	Building on the UN80 Initiative and the change proposals approved as part of the 2026 programme budget, and having undertaken spending reviews informed by updated methodology and standard operating procedures, the component will continue to support the identification of efficiencies that enable Secretariat entities to enhance the overall effectiveness of the Organization, including the pursuit of synergies and structural, operational and functional efficiencies, while safeguarding mandated functions.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.94	The lesson for the component was that, while efficiencies were identified across Secretariat entities as part of the approved 2026 programme budget, prioritizing efficiencies requires a sustained and focused approach. In applying the lesson, the component will consider meaningful ways of providing qualitative and quantifiable information on entities’ efforts to foster a culture of efficiency and cost‑effectiveness.

Component 2 – Field operations finance – Objective

29A.70	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to ensure effective and efficient deliberations by Member States on the results to which the field and other Secretariat entities contribute, and on the resources required for the work of those entities in line with their mandates, and to ensure the effective and efficient management of resources.
Strategy
29A.58	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Ensure the proper application of the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, adapt policies relating to financial matters in response to emerging issues and strengthen the system of internal controls in support of the efficient management of resources in the context of the decentralized delegation of authority framework;

(b)	Provide effective support to the Committee on Contributions, ensure the timely issuance of assessments, provide up-to-date information on the status of assessed contributions and closely monitor and forecast the collection of assessments and manage cash outflows on the basis of liquidity forecasts;

(c)	Prepare International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS)-compliant financial statements for the Organization, focusing on transparency, by incorporating a financial discussion and an analysis statement, as well as ensuring that updates to accounting standards are operationalized and that all entities are appropriately guided in year-end requirements for the preparation of financial statements and are supported in the establishment, management and reporting of the extrabudgetary funds of the Organization;
(d)	Manage financial risks through sound financial processes, investment management, global cash management and global banking management;

(e)	Coordinate and manage the group health and life insurance programmes and provide services to the Advisory Board on Compensation Claims and the United Nations Claims Board, and administer related compensation payments;
(f)	Provide governance of finance master data to ensure the integrity, standardization and harmonization of data across the enterprise resource planning system;
(g)	Provide procedures, guidance and support for Umoja finance processes to ensure effective and efficient financial management.
29A.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Prudent financial management of the resources entrusted to the Secretariat;
(b)	A strengthened system of internal controls that supports better programme performance management and compliance with the regulatory framework;
(c)	Increased transparency and simplification of financial processes and reporting;
(d)	Effective and efficient financial procedures;
(e)	Better positioning of the Organization to respond to external shocks.

29A.71	To contribute to the objective, the component will provide strategic guidance on budgeting and financial stewardship and develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard, in particular:

(a)	Provide strategic guidance on financial management and financing issues, especially on cross-cutting initiatives affecting field operations, including guidance on formulating human resources requirements, corporate initiatives, financial strength and liquidity and strengthening of internal controls, and timely, high-quality, simplified and action-oriented responses to managers in the field; monitor the liquidity of individual special accounts; and oversee cash-flow and operating reserves for field operations;
(b)	Provide strategic guidance on global property management and real estate services, including oversight of major construction projects and long-term capital planning;

(c)	Provide expert advice and guidance on property management and review financial data for accurate recording and for compliance with IPSAS and the established property management performance framework for Secretariat entities, and ensure the central supervision of projects, including by incorporating risk management principles and lessons learned in decision-making.

29A.72	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in client entities effectively and efficiently managing their resources within the approved levels, with improved accuracy in forecasts and proposals.
29A.85	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Issue strategic guidance on the preparation of the proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals and engage with Member States from the early stages of the budget process;

(b)	Finalize and present to the legislative bodies the Secretary-General’s annual proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals, the budget performance reports and other reports on budgetary matters, including statements of programme budget implications and revised programme budget proposals, in accordance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, the Regulations and Rules Governing Programme Planning, the Programme Aspects of the Budget, the Monitoring of Implementation and the Methods of Evaluation and applicable resolutions of the General Assembly;

(c)	Provide information to Member States on the potential budgetary implications of draft resolutions of the General Assembly and its Main Committees, the Economic and Social Council, the Human Rights Council and other legislative organs;

(d)	Provide substantive services on programme planning and budgetary matters to the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly, the Committee for Programme and Coordination and the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions and provide online tools accessible to Member States in order to facilitate deliberations;

(e)	Provide strategic guidance on results-based management, budgeting and financial stewardship, including on managing spending authorities, develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard and provide advice on all budgetary matters.
29A.86	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased transparency and strengthened dialogue between the Secretariat and Member States;
(b)	Efficient and effective implementation of programmes while ensuring the prudent use of resources;
(c)	Improved accuracy of programme budgets.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved access to consolidated cost-recovery information for decision-making by Secretariat entities

29A.60	Cost-recovery activities across the Secretariat are recorded across multiple Umoja modules, including funds management, accounts receivable, general ledger, controlling, sales and distribution, and payroll. The absence of integrated reporting meant that entities relied on fragmented data sources and manual extraction, making it difficult to efficiently monitor fund balances, track revenue, assess receivables or evaluate performance. Following repeated recommendations from oversight bodies for strengthened monitoring, the component led a multi-year effort culminating in 2025 with the deployment of a cost-recovery dashboard in Umoja Analytics. The dashboard integrates data across six modules into a single platform, providing consolidated analysis of fund balance movements, revenue by client and cost-recovery method, receivables ageing and payment patterns, and budget consumption against revenue and cash collected. The Assistant Secretary-General for Programme Planning, Finance and Budget, Controller, as well as heads of entities, service providers and clients can now access streamlined, actionable cost-recovery insights from their own operational perspective, replacing what previously required consulting multiple disconnected reports.
29A.61	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.15).

children.text: Strengthened oversight and accountability in property management

29A.73	In 2025, the component strengthened the organizational performance management framework through the establishment of a consolidated data quality report, underpinned by key performance indicators covering critical data fields, to support entities in identifying data inconsistencies in property records. The indicators focused on the accuracy and timeliness of Umoja data, including the correct reflection of property status across the lifecycle, such as assets issued into operational use versus those held in warehouse, thereby reinforcing accountability for data quality at the entity level.

29A.74	Introduced towards the end of the year, the data quality report provided initial visibility over areas of potential inefficiency in property management processes and laid the foundation for more targeted corrective actions by entities. The framework supported early identification of data gaps, strengthened oversight and reporting and established a basis for more informed management decision-making going forward.

children.text: Successful conclusion of budgetary deliberations on the UN80 initiative within a condensed timeline

29A.87	Following the launch of the UN80 Initiative by the Secretary-General in March 2025 to build a stronger and more effective Organization that is better equipped to deliver on its mandates, the report on the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period (A/80/400) was prepared and presented for review by legislative bodies in a condensed period. The proposals resulted from the engagement of all Secretariat entities led and coordinated by the subprogramme, in particular the component, in its lead role for the programme budget. The General Assembly successfully concluded its deliberations on 30 December 2025.

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved client experience with United Nations global payment systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29A.62	The component’s work contributed to 1,850 individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment, exceeding the planned target of 1,000 clients.
29A.63	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.III).
Figure 29A.III
Performance measure: number of individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment (annual)
Result 2: implementation of approved updates to the International Public Sector Accounting Standards and impact analysis of upcoming changes – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29A.64	The component’s work in integrated system enhancements, authoritative policy documents, Organization-wide lease information capture and a comprehensive guidance and training suite ensured effective implementation of significant IPSAS updates in 2025 and contributed to full audit assurance and no audit recommendations raised during the year, which met the planned target.
29A.65	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.IV).
Figure 29A.IV
Performance measure: number of audit recommendations related to implementation of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (annual)
Note: The financial statements of the United Nations, volume I, for the year ended 31 December 2025 will be audited in April and May 2026.
Result 3: strengthened financial management regulatory framework through newly established and updated policy instruments – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.66	As part of ongoing efforts to strengthen the financial management regulatory framework, promote consistent understanding and application of policies and enhance accountability across Secretariat operations, the component has been conducting a comprehensive review of a compendium of policy instruments, with a view to issuing new and updated key policies and guidelines as applicable.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.67	The lesson for the component was the importance of ensuring that entities receive appropriate technical support and training related to policy updates to ensure harmonization and correct common understanding of the instruments. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will develop and issue additional operational guidelines on an ongoing basis, incorporating feedback received from entities, and provide continuous training to support consistent and effective implementation. This will further support the effective management of financial resources and provide the necessary flexibility in complex operations.

children.text: Result 3: greater transparency and efficiency in the formulation of budgetary requirements for special political missions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.80	The component makes efforts to identify efficiency gains in the budgetary preparation process for the special political missions while ensuring effective mandate implementation, including by applying a data-driven approach in formulating budgetary requirements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.81	The lesson for the component was the importance of building and strengthening a culture of efficiency for the use of resources for the implementation of mandates. In applying the lesson, the component will, in line with General Assembly resolution 80/243, facilitate mutual learning across missions in identifying, tracking, quantifying and reporting on efficiency initiatives across post and non-post resources. To enhance reporting, reports of the Secretary-General will include additional information on efficiency gains, including a comparison of projected efficiencies made at the outset of the budget period and the actual realized efficiencies.

29A.89	The component’s work contributed to Member States from four Main Committees having access to information at the start of their session on the programme budget implications process through a detailed guide available on the portal, which did not meet the planned target of Member States from six Main Committees having access to information. children.text: Result 3: greater efficiency reflected in budgetary requirements across Secretariat entities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.93	Building on the UN80 Initiative and the change proposals approved as part of the 2026 programme budget, and having undertaken spending reviews informed by updated methodology and standard operating procedures, the component will continue to support the identification of efficiencies that enable Secretariat entities to enhance the overall effectiveness of the Organization, including the pursuit of synergies and structural, operational and functional efficiencies, while safeguarding mandated functions.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.94	The lesson for the component was that, while efficiencies were identified across Secretariat entities as part of the approved 2026 programme budget, prioritizing efficiencies requires a sustained and focused approach. In applying the lesson, the component will consider meaningful ways of providing qualitative and quantifiable information on entities’ efforts to foster a culture of efficiency and cost‑effectiveness.

children.text: a	10 in A/80/6 (Sect.29A)
Component 3 – Programme planning and budgeting – Objective

29A.84	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to ensure the effective and efficient deliberations by Member States on the results to which the Secretariat contributes, on the translation of mandates into programme plans and on the resources required for the work of the Secretariat in line with its mandates, and to ensure the effective and efficient management of resources and transparent reporting thereon.
Strategy
29A.58	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Ensure the proper application of the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, adapt policies relating to financial matters in response to emerging issues and strengthen the system of internal controls in support of the efficient management of resources in the context of the decentralized delegation of authority framework;

(b)	Provide effective support to the Committee on Contributions, ensure the timely issuance of assessments, provide up-to-date information on the status of assessed contributions and closely monitor and forecast the collection of assessments and manage cash outflows on the basis of liquidity forecasts;

(c)	Prepare International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS)-compliant financial statements for the Organization, focusing on transparency, by incorporating a financial discussion and an analysis statement, as well as ensuring that updates to accounting standards are operationalized and that all entities are appropriately guided in year-end requirements for the preparation of financial statements and are supported in the establishment, management and reporting of the extrabudgetary funds of the Organization;
(d)	Manage financial risks through sound financial processes, investment management, global cash management and global banking management;

(e)	Coordinate and manage the group health and life insurance programmes and provide services to the Advisory Board on Compensation Claims and the United Nations Claims Board, and administer related compensation payments;
(f)	Provide governance of finance master data to ensure the integrity, standardization and harmonization of data across the enterprise resource planning system;
(g)	Provide procedures, guidance and support for Umoja finance processes to ensure effective and efficient financial management.
29A.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Prudent financial management of the resources entrusted to the Secretariat;
(b)	A strengthened system of internal controls that supports better programme performance management and compliance with the regulatory framework;
(c)	Increased transparency and simplification of financial processes and reporting;
(d)	Effective and efficient financial procedures;
(e)	Better positioning of the Organization to respond to external shocks.

29A.71	To contribute to the objective, the component will provide strategic guidance on budgeting and financial stewardship and develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard, in particular:

(a)	Provide strategic guidance on financial management and financing issues, especially on cross-cutting initiatives affecting field operations, including guidance on formulating human resources requirements, corporate initiatives, financial strength and liquidity and strengthening of internal controls, and timely, high-quality, simplified and action-oriented responses to managers in the field; monitor the liquidity of individual special accounts; and oversee cash-flow and operating reserves for field operations;
(b)	Provide strategic guidance on global property management and real estate services, including oversight of major construction projects and long-term capital planning;

(c)	Provide expert advice and guidance on property management and review financial data for accurate recording and for compliance with IPSAS and the established property management performance framework for Secretariat entities, and ensure the central supervision of projects, including by incorporating risk management principles and lessons learned in decision-making.

29A.72	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in client entities effectively and efficiently managing their resources within the approved levels, with improved accuracy in forecasts and proposals.
29A.85	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Issue strategic guidance on the preparation of the proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals and engage with Member States from the early stages of the budget process;

(b)	Finalize and present to the legislative bodies the Secretary-General’s annual proposed programme budget and the proposed budget of the International Residual Mechanism for Criminal Tribunals, the budget performance reports and other reports on budgetary matters, including statements of programme budget implications and revised programme budget proposals, in accordance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, the Regulations and Rules Governing Programme Planning, the Programme Aspects of the Budget, the Monitoring of Implementation and the Methods of Evaluation and applicable resolutions of the General Assembly;

(c)	Provide information to Member States on the potential budgetary implications of draft resolutions of the General Assembly and its Main Committees, the Economic and Social Council, the Human Rights Council and other legislative organs;

(d)	Provide substantive services on programme planning and budgetary matters to the Fifth Committee of the General Assembly, the Committee for Programme and Coordination and the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions and provide online tools accessible to Member States in order to facilitate deliberations;

(e)	Provide strategic guidance on results-based management, budgeting and financial stewardship, including on managing spending authorities, develop policies, methodologies and tools in that regard and provide advice on all budgetary matters.
29A.86	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased transparency and strengthened dialogue between the Secretariat and Member States;
(b)	Efficient and effective implementation of programmes while ensuring the prudent use of resources;
(c)	Improved accuracy of programme budgets.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved access to consolidated cost-recovery information for decision-making by Secretariat entities

29A.60	Cost-recovery activities across the Secretariat are recorded across multiple Umoja modules, including funds management, accounts receivable, general ledger, controlling, sales and distribution, and payroll. The absence of integrated reporting meant that entities relied on fragmented data sources and manual extraction, making it difficult to efficiently monitor fund balances, track revenue, assess receivables or evaluate performance. Following repeated recommendations from oversight bodies for strengthened monitoring, the component led a multi-year effort culminating in 2025 with the deployment of a cost-recovery dashboard in Umoja Analytics. The dashboard integrates data across six modules into a single platform, providing consolidated analysis of fund balance movements, revenue by client and cost-recovery method, receivables ageing and payment patterns, and budget consumption against revenue and cash collected. The Assistant Secretary-General for Programme Planning, Finance and Budget, Controller, as well as heads of entities, service providers and clients can now access streamlined, actionable cost-recovery insights from their own operational perspective, replacing what previously required consulting multiple disconnected reports.
29A.61	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.15).

children.text: Strengthened oversight and accountability in property management

29A.73	In 2025, the component strengthened the organizational performance management framework through the establishment of a consolidated data quality report, underpinned by key performance indicators covering critical data fields, to support entities in identifying data inconsistencies in property records. The indicators focused on the accuracy and timeliness of Umoja data, including the correct reflection of property status across the lifecycle, such as assets issued into operational use versus those held in warehouse, thereby reinforcing accountability for data quality at the entity level.

29A.74	Introduced towards the end of the year, the data quality report provided initial visibility over areas of potential inefficiency in property management processes and laid the foundation for more targeted corrective actions by entities. The framework supported early identification of data gaps, strengthened oversight and reporting and established a basis for more informed management decision-making going forward.

children.text: Successful conclusion of budgetary deliberations on the UN80 initiative within a condensed timeline

29A.87	Following the launch of the UN80 Initiative by the Secretary-General in March 2025 to build a stronger and more effective Organization that is better equipped to deliver on its mandates, the report on the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period (A/80/400) was prepared and presented for review by legislative bodies in a condensed period. The proposals resulted from the engagement of all Secretariat entities led and coordinated by the subprogramme, in particular the component, in its lead role for the programme budget. The General Assembly successfully concluded its deliberations on 30 December 2025.
