children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved client experience with United Nations global payment systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29A.62	The component’s work contributed to 1,850 individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment, exceeding the planned target of 1,000 clients.
29A.63	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.III).
Figure 29A.III
Performance measure: number of individual clients using debit cards and digital wallets as a preferred mode of payment (annual)
Result 2: implementation of approved updates to the International Public Sector Accounting Standards and impact analysis of upcoming changes – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29A.64	The component’s work in integrated system enhancements, authoritative policy documents, Organization-wide lease information capture and a comprehensive guidance and training suite ensured effective implementation of significant IPSAS updates in 2025 and contributed to full audit assurance and no audit recommendations raised during the year, which met the planned target.
29A.65	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.IV).
Figure 29A.IV
Performance measure: number of audit recommendations related to implementation of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (annual)
Note: The financial statements of the United Nations, volume I, for the year ended 31 December 2025 will be audited in April and May 2026.
Result 3: strengthened financial management regulatory framework through newly established and updated policy instruments – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.66	As part of ongoing efforts to strengthen the financial management regulatory framework, promote consistent understanding and application of policies and enhance accountability across Secretariat operations, the component has been conducting a comprehensive review of a compendium of policy instruments, with a view to issuing new and updated key policies and guidelines as applicable.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.67	The lesson for the component was the importance of ensuring that entities receive appropriate technical support and training related to policy updates to ensure harmonization and correct common understanding of the instruments. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will develop and issue additional operational guidelines on an ongoing basis, incorporating feedback received from entities, and provide continuous training to support consistent and effective implementation. This will further support the effective management of financial resources and provide the necessary flexibility in complex operations.

children.text: Result 3: greater transparency and efficiency in the formulation of budgetary requirements for special political missions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.80	The component makes efforts to identify efficiency gains in the budgetary preparation process for the special political missions while ensuring effective mandate implementation, including by applying a data-driven approach in formulating budgetary requirements.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.81	The lesson for the component was the importance of building and strengthening a culture of efficiency for the use of resources for the implementation of mandates. In applying the lesson, the component will, in line with General Assembly resolution 80/243, facilitate mutual learning across missions in identifying, tracking, quantifying and reporting on efficiency initiatives across post and non-post resources. To enhance reporting, reports of the Secretary-General will include additional information on efficiency gains, including a comparison of projected efficiencies made at the outset of the budget period and the actual realized efficiencies.

children.text: Result 3: greater efficiency reflected in budgetary requirements across Secretariat entities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.93	Building on the UN80 Initiative and the change proposals approved as part of the 2026 programme budget, and having undertaken spending reviews informed by updated methodology and standard operating procedures, the component will continue to support the identification of efficiencies that enable Secretariat entities to enhance the overall effectiveness of the Organization, including the pursuit of synergies and structural, operational and functional efficiencies, while safeguarding mandated functions.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.94	The lesson for the component was that, while efficiencies were identified across Secretariat entities as part of the approved 2026 programme budget, prioritizing efficiencies requires a sustained and focused approach. In applying the lesson, the component will consider meaningful ways of providing qualitative and quantifiable information on entities’ efforts to foster a culture of efficiency and cost‑effectiveness.

A.	Facilitation of the intergovernmental process and expert bodies
Parliamentary documentation (number of documents)
1.	Annual financial reports to the General Assembly
2.	Reports on multi-year payment plans and the financial situation of the United Nations to the General Assembly
3.	Report on improving the financial situation of the United Nations to the General Assembly

12.	Donor financial reports
C.	Substantive deliverables

Consultation, advice and advocacy: advice to donors on the financial arrangements and status of voluntary contributions, as required; and ad hoc information on the scale of assessments, peacekeeping financing and the status of contributions.

E.	Enabling deliverables

Subprogramme 3 – Human resources
Component 1 – Global strategy and policy – Objective

29A.97	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to ensure a decentralized, agile, field-focused management paradigm for the effective and transparent management of human resources and a high-performing, diverse and engaged workforce.
Strategy

29A.98	To contribute to the objective, the component will further develop, update and enhance the recruitment system to ensure that it is effective, fair, efficient and agile and to enable the United Nations to have a diverse workforce and foster a culture of ethics, integrity, transparency and accountability in the Organization. Specifically, the component will:
(a)	Steer efforts for equitable geographical distribution under the system of desirable ranges, in accordance with General Assembly resolution 77/278;

(b)	Provide strategic guidance on human resources supported by an enabling policy framework to fulfil entities’ mandates, in which team, individual and organizational performance are aligned and human resources risks are transparently and effectively managed; and foster innovation as a means of improving programmatic delivery of human resources in support of cultural change across the Organization;

(c)	Establish strategic policy imperatives anchored in guidance and feedback from entities, in particular from the field, continue to provide authoritative policy interpretation to ensure consistency, fairness and the equitable treatment of staff in the global Secretariat, and oversee the setting of salaries and benefits for locally recruited staff members by applying methodologies established by the International Civil Service Commission;

(d)	Actively engage on all aspects of relations between staff and management and work with other organizations of the United Nations common system on salaries and allowances to advance the work of the High-level Committee on Management and the Human Resources Network of CEB, and through the International Civil Service Commission, including by leading the implementation of the United Nations workplace mental health and well-being strategy across the United Nations system;

(e)	Further develop efforts towards mutual recognition over time, which calls for entities within the United Nations common system to operate according to the principle of mutual recognition of best practices in terms of policies and procedures, and lead efforts to enhance harmonization and improve efficiency in the United Nations system;

(f)	Improve talent management in the Organization and, in particular, work towards implementing the new approach to mobility and organizational learning, developing a new staff selection system that is effective, fair, efficient and agile, and that enables the Organization to have a diverse workforce, adopting approaches that incorporate new values and behaviours into talent management and strengthening the performance management framework;

(g)	In line with action 45 (a) of the Pact for the Future, engage in talent management actions, such as the upskilling and reskilling of staff or the creation of new job profiles, to enable the workforce of the Organization to meet the future demands identified through strategic workforce planning, and to attract and retain the best talent with due regard to geographical representation and gender parity priorities;
(h)	Provide workforce data to external stakeholders for analyses of staff and affiliated personnel demographics, Member State representation and workforce trends;

(i)	Manage and provide monitoring, coordination and technical support to ensure the effective and coherent programmatic and operational implementation of the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and provide targeted support to strengthen the mainstreaming of disability inclusion, capacity-building, reasonable accommodation and accessibility across the Secretariat.
29A.99	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A culture of staff mobility that accelerates the mobility process across the Organization, in particular movements between Headquarters and field duty stations;

(b)	Talent acquisition focused on current workforce capability needs, anticipation of future needs and enhancement of existing talent through learning and development, mobility and effective performance management and career satisfaction support;
(c)	Gender parity at all levels;

(d)	Recruitment of staff on as wide a geographical basis as possible, a reduced number of unrepresented and underrepresented Member States, and progress made on bringing as many Member States as possible within their desirable range;

(e)	Improved institutional capacity on disability inclusion, reasonable accommodation and accessibility across the Organization, enabling engagement with and inclusion of persons with disabilities in all areas of the Organization’s work and support to Member States.
29A.111	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Deploy and use a global case management system and identify lessons learned, including in relation to critical incidents involving serious reputational risks affecting the Organization’s core values and norms, and develop tools and guidance material for senior leaders;

(b)	Engage in capacity-building, including through the online information-sharing platform ALD Connect, direct outreach to senior managers and the ongoing development of tools and guidance materials to support senior managers in carrying out their responsibilities and exercising their authority;

(c)	Review disciplinary matters for sanctioning purposes, represent the Secretary-General before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal regarding appeals against administrative decisions, continue to expand screening for integrity for the Secretariat, and continue to review and update policies relating to the conduct of personnel.
29A.112	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced oversight across the Secretariat and monitoring of matters that affect the reputation of the Organization, and the timely review and handling of misconduct matters;
(b)	Well-informed decision-making by senior managers in relation to the management of personnel;
(c)	An enhanced culture of individual accountability.
Programme performance in 2025
Staff members, managers and senior leadership navigated organizational change

29A.100	In 2025, organizational change across the Secretariat heightened the need to provide comprehensive, timely and consistent information to staff, managers and senior leaders. The component contributed by consolidating staff-facing guidance and updates through established channels and a staff support framework that has been updated regularly as new information becomes available, alongside a feedback mechanism enabling staff to share suggestions.

children.text: Expanded use of screening tools for conduct and discipline matters

29A.113	The component contributed to and supported initiatives to strengthen accountability in matters related to conduct and discipline across the global Secretariat. In response to General Assembly resolutions 77/280 and 78/253 and through consultations with stakeholders, the scope of ClearCheck was expanded to include all substantiated forms of serious misconduct including racism and racial discrimination matters in addition to sexual harassment and sexual exploitation and abuse.

children.text: Result 3: comprehensive young-talent approach enhancing the Organization’s ability to attract, develop and retain early-career talent – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.106	The Organization has in place several established entry pathways to recruit and retain young talent, including the young professionals programme, Junior Professional Officer programmes and internships, which contribute to efforts to rejuvenate the workforce, including as reflected in General Assembly resolution 77/278. Experience with these programmes has highlighted the value of a more coordinated approach to maximize their collective impact, particularly in relation to strategic outreach, partnerships, workforce planning and longer-term talent management.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.107	The lesson for the component was the need to strengthen coordination between the existing young talent programmes and provide systematic and structured support to enhance their long-term contribution to workforce diversity. In applying this lesson, the component will, in a phased manner, work towards a more comprehensive and coordinated approach to young talent by better linking existing programmes, strengthened strategic talent outreach and partnerships, supporting workforce planning and enhancing structured talent management support. The component will lead coordination efforts with relevant implementing entities.

children.text: Result 3: enhanced accountability through efficiency and consistency in the review and disposition of disciplinary cases – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.119	The component has been leveraging the application of available electronic tools to identify areas within the disciplinary review process that can be optimized with the available resources to improve the efficiency in the processing of disciplinary matters before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal on behalf of the Secretary-General.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.120	The lesson for the component was that leveraging emerging technologies can significantly enhance the efficiency and consistency of legal research across both public and confidential information streams. In applying this lesson, the component will adapt tools—develop solutions for public sources and a secure internal system for confidential and strictly confidential materials—to facilitate the greater disposition of cases, with the aim of increasing accountability. Going forward, the component will expand the use of these tools to streamline research processes and reduce manual workloads.

children.text: Component 2 – Administrative law – Objective
29A.110	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to strengthen accountability in line with the standards of conduct by all categories of personnel.
Strategy

29A.98	To contribute to the objective, the component will further develop, update and enhance the recruitment system to ensure that it is effective, fair, efficient and agile and to enable the United Nations to have a diverse workforce and foster a culture of ethics, integrity, transparency and accountability in the Organization. Specifically, the component will:
(a)	Steer efforts for equitable geographical distribution under the system of desirable ranges, in accordance with General Assembly resolution 77/278;

(b)	Provide strategic guidance on human resources supported by an enabling policy framework to fulfil entities’ mandates, in which team, individual and organizational performance are aligned and human resources risks are transparently and effectively managed; and foster innovation as a means of improving programmatic delivery of human resources in support of cultural change across the Organization;

(c)	Establish strategic policy imperatives anchored in guidance and feedback from entities, in particular from the field, continue to provide authoritative policy interpretation to ensure consistency, fairness and the equitable treatment of staff in the global Secretariat, and oversee the setting of salaries and benefits for locally recruited staff members by applying methodologies established by the International Civil Service Commission;

(d)	Actively engage on all aspects of relations between staff and management and work with other organizations of the United Nations common system on salaries and allowances to advance the work of the High-level Committee on Management and the Human Resources Network of CEB, and through the International Civil Service Commission, including by leading the implementation of the United Nations workplace mental health and well-being strategy across the United Nations system;

(e)	Further develop efforts towards mutual recognition over time, which calls for entities within the United Nations common system to operate according to the principle of mutual recognition of best practices in terms of policies and procedures, and lead efforts to enhance harmonization and improve efficiency in the United Nations system;

(f)	Improve talent management in the Organization and, in particular, work towards implementing the new approach to mobility and organizational learning, developing a new staff selection system that is effective, fair, efficient and agile, and that enables the Organization to have a diverse workforce, adopting approaches that incorporate new values and behaviours into talent management and strengthening the performance management framework;

(g)	In line with action 45 (a) of the Pact for the Future, engage in talent management actions, such as the upskilling and reskilling of staff or the creation of new job profiles, to enable the workforce of the Organization to meet the future demands identified through strategic workforce planning, and to attract and retain the best talent with due regard to geographical representation and gender parity priorities;
(h)	Provide workforce data to external stakeholders for analyses of staff and affiliated personnel demographics, Member State representation and workforce trends;

(i)	Manage and provide monitoring, coordination and technical support to ensure the effective and coherent programmatic and operational implementation of the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and provide targeted support to strengthen the mainstreaming of disability inclusion, capacity-building, reasonable accommodation and accessibility across the Secretariat.
29A.99	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A culture of staff mobility that accelerates the mobility process across the Organization, in particular movements between Headquarters and field duty stations;

(b)	Talent acquisition focused on current workforce capability needs, anticipation of future needs and enhancement of existing talent through learning and development, mobility and effective performance management and career satisfaction support;
(c)	Gender parity at all levels;

(d)	Recruitment of staff on as wide a geographical basis as possible, a reduced number of unrepresented and underrepresented Member States, and progress made on bringing as many Member States as possible within their desirable range;

(e)	Improved institutional capacity on disability inclusion, reasonable accommodation and accessibility across the Organization, enabling engagement with and inclusion of persons with disabilities in all areas of the Organization’s work and support to Member States.
29A.111	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Deploy and use a global case management system and identify lessons learned, including in relation to critical incidents involving serious reputational risks affecting the Organization’s core values and norms, and develop tools and guidance material for senior leaders;

(b)	Engage in capacity-building, including through the online information-sharing platform ALD Connect, direct outreach to senior managers and the ongoing development of tools and guidance materials to support senior managers in carrying out their responsibilities and exercising their authority;

(c)	Review disciplinary matters for sanctioning purposes, represent the Secretary-General before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal regarding appeals against administrative decisions, continue to expand screening for integrity for the Secretariat, and continue to review and update policies relating to the conduct of personnel.
29A.112	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced oversight across the Secretariat and monitoring of matters that affect the reputation of the Organization, and the timely review and handling of misconduct matters;
(b)	Well-informed decision-making by senior managers in relation to the management of personnel;
(c)	An enhanced culture of individual accountability.
Programme performance in 2025
Staff members, managers and senior leadership navigated organizational change

29A.100	In 2025, organizational change across the Secretariat heightened the need to provide comprehensive, timely and consistent information to staff, managers and senior leaders. The component contributed by consolidating staff-facing guidance and updates through established channels and a staff support framework that has been updated regularly as new information becomes available, alongside a feedback mechanism enabling staff to share suggestions.

children.text: Result 3: comprehensive young-talent approach enhancing the Organization’s ability to attract, develop and retain early-career talent – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.106	The Organization has in place several established entry pathways to recruit and retain young talent, including the young professionals programme, Junior Professional Officer programmes and internships, which contribute to efforts to rejuvenate the workforce, including as reflected in General Assembly resolution 77/278. Experience with these programmes has highlighted the value of a more coordinated approach to maximize their collective impact, particularly in relation to strategic outreach, partnerships, workforce planning and longer-term talent management.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.107	The lesson for the component was the need to strengthen coordination between the existing young talent programmes and provide systematic and structured support to enhance their long-term contribution to workforce diversity. In applying this lesson, the component will, in a phased manner, work towards a more comprehensive and coordinated approach to young talent by better linking existing programmes, strengthened strategic talent outreach and partnerships, supporting workforce planning and enhancing structured talent management support. The component will lead coordination efforts with relevant implementing entities.

children.text: Result 3: enhanced accountability through efficiency and consistency in the review and disposition of disciplinary cases – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.119	The component has been leveraging the application of available electronic tools to identify areas within the disciplinary review process that can be optimized with the available resources to improve the efficiency in the processing of disciplinary matters before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal on behalf of the Secretary-General.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.120	The lesson for the component was that leveraging emerging technologies can significantly enhance the efficiency and consistency of legal research across both public and confidential information streams. In applying this lesson, the component will adapt tools—develop solutions for public sources and a secure internal system for confidential and strictly confidential materials—to facilitate the greater disposition of cases, with the aim of increasing accountability. Going forward, the component will expand the use of these tools to streamline research processes and reduce manual workloads.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Business transformation and accountability
Objective

29A.123	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure a results-oriented, data-driven, agile Organization that is efficient, accountable, transparent, compliant and driven by lessons learned and continuous improvements.
Strategy
29A.124	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Focus on simplifying the Organization’s systems of accountability by evaluating overall organizational performance trends, assessing the alignment of roles and responsibilities and monitoring the exercise of delegated authority, monitoring the senior managers’ compacts, mainstreaming the use of evaluation as part of the programme planning cycle, and coordinating the Secretariat’s interaction with oversight bodies and identifying trends in their recommendations;
(b)	Carry out capacity-building activities to support all United Nations entities in identifying, assessing, evaluating and controlling risk, using an enterprise risk management approach;

(c)	Support the implementation of the data strategy of the Secretariat, enable entities to have access to management data from Secretariat systems and to gain access to or design analytical tools supporting data-driven decision-making, and lead and coordinate a portfolio of business transformation and innovation projects.
29A.125	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A reinforced accountability culture in the Organization in the areas of risk awareness, monitoring performance and the exercise of delegated authority;
(b)	Greater use of enterprise data and data analytics in decision-making and programme delivery;
(c)	Teams across the Secretariat adopting agile methodology and innovation and strengthening new collaboration practices using online platforms.
Programme performance in 2025
Problem-solving using generative artificial intelligence to improve efficiency

29A.126	The subprogramme began experimenting with the potential of generative artificial intelligence to achieve efficiency gains in core business processes after the software was made available in 2025. A series of pilots identified significant opportunities to maintain the timely provision of information/advice to clients in spite of the recruitment freeze. For example, in the fourth quarter of 2025, the subprogramme advanced its development and use of generative artificial intelligence tools within the United Nations’ information technology environment. The tool supports structured information retrieval across internal records and guidance, enabling comprehensive and traceable research to inform requests for briefings, managing meetings and responding to intergovernmental and oversight body enquiries.
29A.127	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.36).
Table 29A.36
Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)

2025 (actual)

Use of generative artificial intelligence enabled the reengineering of 2 pilot business processes in the areas of providing briefings for senior managers and responding to enquiries from stakeholders, thereby maintaining the timely provision of information/advice to clients and increasing the consistency of outputs. Lessons from the initial pilots are informing how other entities perform similar processes

The target was not met due to the ongoing liquidity challenges of the Organization, as the funds approved to enable evaluation experts to support small entities to undertake evaluations were not made available.

29A.130	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the availability of 4 new and/or enhanced features or functionalities available on the management dashboard in support of improved decision-making by programme managers, which met the cumulative planned target of 21.
29A.131	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29A.VII).
Figure 29A.VII
Performance measure: number of new and/or enhanced features or functionalities available on the management dashboard (cumulative)
Result 3: timely implementation of oversight bodies’ recommendations – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29A.132	The Secretariat must ensure the timely implementation of oversight recommendations. In 2025, the subprogramme undertook an audit process review and established a dedicated capacity to monitor and support entities in engaging with oversight bodies and closing outstanding recommendations. The subprogramme’s enhancements to the management dashboard now allow for more effective monitoring by including recommendations from both the Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) and the Board of Auditors from 2021 and 2025, respectively, thereby providing entities with centralized access to this information to support timely action and operational improvement.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.133	The lesson for the subprogramme was that capacity-building across the Secretariat, combined with close engagement with the oversight bodies, is essential for improving implementation rates. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to provide targeted support, such as guidance, working sessions and one-on-one training, to help entities strengthen capacity and resolve outstanding and long-standing recommendations.

children.text: Subprogramme 5 – Addressing racism in the workplace
Objective

29A.136	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure a workplace free from racism and racial discrimination, in which every staff member feels valued and respected and is treated with dignity.
Strategy
29A.137	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Implement the strategic action plan by driving progress towards a racism-free Secretariat through oversight, coordination and monitoring of all anti-racism initiatives;
(b)	Review and address existing or potential racial bias in institutional policies and practices and promote data transparency;
©	Promote advocacy, awareness-raising and training programmes to support personnel and foster an organizational culture to address racism;

(d)	Strengthen accountability mechanisms within the Organization by providing expertise to United Nations system entities on evolving practices and developments relating to the investigation and adjudication of racial discrimination matters;
©	Address institutional and systemic racism by establishing and reinforcing leadership accountability mechanisms;

(f)	Advance system-wide anti-racism initiatives by fostering collaboration and synergies with United Nations system entities and other relevant partners, including through the exchange of best practices and lessons learned.
29A.138	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	An institutionalized anti-racism culture fully integrated into the policies, procedures and practices within the Organization, upholding a zero-tolerance approach to racism and racial discrimination;
(b)	The empowerment of staff and the prevention of racial bias in the institutional policies and practices of the Organization;
©	Increased trust in internal systems and processes of administration of justice to prevent, address, mediate, investigate and adjudicate fairly allegations of racism and to provide racial justice.
Programme performance in 2025
Addressing interpersonal, institutional, and systemic forms of racism in the Organization

29A.139	The subprogramme continued to implement the series of initiatives that had been launched to target interpersonal, institutional and systemic forms of racism within the Organization. The subprogramme convened the inaugural United Nations Conference on Addressing Racism and Promoting Dignity for All at the Secretariat in June 2025; continued entity-level engagement through dialogues, conversations and town hall meetings; and launched the Secretariat’s first online course entitled “Addressing racism and racial discrimination in the workplace” in English and French. The subprogramme made structural advances through the completion of a framework for the collection of race-related data, in line with General Assembly resolution 78/253. The subprogramme also worked to strengthen accountability mechanisms through progress on amending the Secretary-General’s bulletin on addressing discrimination, harassment, including sexual harassment, and abuse of authority (ST/SGB/2019/8), planned to be promulgated in 2026, to include racial discrimination as prohibited conduct.
29A.140	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29A.39).
Table 29A.39
Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)
2024 (actual)

children.text: Planned result for 2027 – Result 1: strengthened accountability systems to address allegations of racism in the workplace – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29A.141	The subprogramme’s work contributed to progress in the review and consultations processes to incorporate a definition of racial discrimination in the revised Secretary-General’s bulletin on addressing discrimination, harassment, including sexual harassment, and abuse of authority (ST/SGB/2019/8), which did not meet the planned target of the revised bulletin to be available in 2025. The target was not met due to prolonged consultation between key stakeholders on the legal definition of racial discrimination as prohibited conduct in the workplace. The revised bulletin is expected to be promulgated in 2026.

children.text: Result 3: Increased knowledge related to addressing racism and racial discrimination in the workplace

29A.145	Since the inception of the Anti-Racism Office, the subprogramme has continued to implement the strategic action plan to make progress in institutionalizing anti-racism culture within the Organization.
Lessons learned and planned change

29A.146	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, to increase awareness and knowledge related to addressing racism and racial discrimination in the workplace, targeted trainings are necessary for specialized functions (e.g. staff counsellors, investigators, conduct and discipline officers, administrative law officers) and key stakeholders (senior leadership, anti-racism advocates, staff unions, affinity groups). In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will promote learning and dialogue through town hall meetings, live talks, brown-bag sessions and conversations; launch an online resource hub to support advocates; roll out a basic awareness course for all personnel; and convene a global conference for anti-racism advocates.
